currentUse: Part of "Superior Farm," a ~3-acre urban-farm and community-hub campus at 6527 Superior Ave. owned by the food-justice nonprofit Food Strong; the 20,000 sq ft former church building stands and is slated for rehabilitation into a community gathering, event, and food-education space, with the former rectory/farmhouse becoming Food Strong's headquarters.
summary: The former Šv. Jurgio (St. George's Lithuanian) church at 6527 Superior Avenue (E. 67th St.) in Cleveland is still standing. After the parish's October 2009 suppression, the Diocese of Cleveland sold the ~2.7-3 acre property to the nonprofit Community Greenhouse Partners, whose purchase agreement was publicly confirmed by a diocesan spokesman in October 2010, for use as an urban farm; the church interior was stripped of its Lithuanian memorials, with artifacts moved to the successor Lithuanian parish and one side altar sold to an Anglican church near Woodinville, WA, and the building spent years largely idle apart from serving as a filming location for the horror film "Little Evil." In September 2024 Community Greenhouse Partners donated the entire property — a 7,000 sq ft farmhouse (the former rectory), the 20,000 sq ft church, gardens, and hoop houses — to fellow Cleveland nonprofit Food Strong, which held a groundbreaking and ribbon-cutting for its "Superior Farm" on May 30, 2025. Food Strong's five-year plan turns the campus into an urban farm and community hub, reimagining the church as an event, gathering, and food-education space with a year-round farmers market and a restored commercial kitchen as an incubator for food entrepreneurs. The parish community itself was merged in October 2009 with the suppressed Our Lady of Perpetual Help parish into a new consolidated St. Casimir (Šv. Kazimiero) parish at 18022 Neff Rd., which remains open in the diocesan directory with administrator Andrew Morkunas.

developments.headline: Parish suppressed and merged into new consolidated St. Casimir parish
developments.detail: The new St. Casimir parish was established in October 2009 from the suppressed St. George Parish and the suppressed Our Lady of Perpetual Help Parish, located at the former Our Lady of Perpetual Help site at 18022 Neff Rd. The Diocese of Cleveland directory lists the parish as established 2009 with Andrew Morkunas as administrator. (Note: this Lithuanian consolidated parish is distinct from the Polish St. Casimir on Sowinski Ave. that won a Vatican appeal and reopened in 2012.)

developments.headline: Diocese agrees to sell property to Community Greenhouse Partners for urban agriculture
developments.detail: Cleveland Scene reported the former St. George Lithuanian Parish property at E. 65th/67th and Superior was being converted into a greenhouse operation by Community Greenhouse Partners, with diocesan spokesman Bob Tayek confirming the Bishop had signed off on the buyer. Freshwater Cleveland reported CGP (represented by Tim Smith) announced an estimated $3.4 million greenhouse project on the site to sell low-cost organic vegetables to low-income families. No sale price was disclosed in either opened article.

sources.title: Community Greenhouse Partners to spend $3.4M on urban farm and greenhouse on vacant Cleveland lot

developments.headline: Interior stripped of Lithuanian artifacts; building later idle after film shoot
developments.detail: Per Global True Lithuania's Cleveland heritage page: the church's Lithuanian details and memorials (Lithuanian freedom fighters, Jurgis Matulaitis) no longer remain inside — many artifacts were relocated to the successor Lithuanian church or its storage, and one side altar without Lithuanian symbols was sold to an Anglican church near Seattle in Woodinville, WA. The same source states the building stood abandoned after a brief stint as a filming location for the horror film 'Little Evil,' and claims the diocese initially sought at least $220,000 for the property before reducing the price to about $11,000 (price claim not corroborated elsewhere).

sources.title: Cleveland, Ohio: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Community Greenhouse Partners donates the property to Food Strong
developments.detail: Food Strong's website states CGP donated the nearly three-acre property — 7,000 sq ft farmhouse, 20,000 sq ft church (formerly St. George's Lithuanian Catholic Church), gardens, and hoop houses — to Food Strong in September. The Euclid Observer covered the nonprofit-to-nonprofit transfer of the farm at Superior and E. 67th on December 1, 2024, quoting Food Strong board member Kevin Ament. The Land later reported CGP selected Food Strong through an RFP process after Food Strong submitted a plan with 15 letters of support.

sources.title: One Local Nonprofit gives an entire farm to another, right here in Cleveland

developments.headline: Food Strong breaks ground on 'Superior Farm' at the former parish campus
developments.detail: Food Strong held a groundbreaking and ribbon-cutting (10:30 AM) on May 30, 2025 at the three-acre former church property, launching a five-year plan for food production and distribution and a multi-organization community hub and education center.

sources.title: NEWS: Food Justice Nonprofit Food Strong Breaks Ground on its New Farm Property

developments.headline: Church building to become community gathering and food-education hub
developments.detail: The Land reported the 1921 church (architect J. Ellsworth Potter) remains a prominent standing structure on E. 67th St. and that Food Strong plans to transform it into 'an event space, a gathering space — a center for cultivating, educating, and connecting,' hosting theater, live music, weddings, craft fairs, and a year-round farmers market, with its commercial kitchen restored as an incubator for food entrepreneurs. Year one of the five-year plan focuses on restoring and expanding the gardens; years 2-5 add housing, vocational training, health services, and recreation.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive's buyer-identity conflict is resolved: the buyer was the nonprofit Community Greenhouse Partners, confirmed by two contemporaneous 2010 news reports (Cleveland Scene, with diocesan spokesman Bob Tayek confirming the bishop signed off on the buyer; Freshwater Cleveland) and by the current owner Food Strong's own account of the CGP-to-Food-Strong chain of title. The archive's "now derelict" note is outdated as a present-day description: while Global True Lithuania described the building as standing abandoned after the 'Little Evil' film shoot, the property was donated to Food Strong in September 2024 and since May 2025 is an actively developing urban-farm campus, with the standing church slated for rehabilitation as a community hub. Archive facts of founding 1895, closure October 2009, merger into the consolidated Šv. Kazimiero parish, and diocesan sale are all confirmed with no contradictions.
gaps: Sale price to Community Greenhouse Partners is unverified: search snippets attribute a $175,000 asking price to a 2010 Cleveland Area History blog post (site unreachable when fetched), while Global True Lithuania claims the diocese cut the price from at least $220,000 to about $11,000; no opened source states the actual closing price or exact deed date (search snippets say December 2010). The successor parish's current Mass schedule (reportedly Sun 11:00 AM in Lithuanian) appeared only in search-result snippets from mass-time aggregators and could not be verified directly — the parish website (saintcasimirparish.org) refused connection; the diocese directory confirms only that the parish is open with administrator Andrew Morkunas. A February 3, 2026 Cleveland North East Design Review agenda reportedly includes a "Food Strong Offices" rehabilitation item at 6527 Superior Ave (Desmone Architects), but the PDF's text could not be extracted to confirm. Caution for future researchers: a widely indexed pbase.com photo gallery titled "Demolition of St. George Lithuanian Catholic Church" documents the church in Shenandoah, PA — not Cleveland; the Cleveland building has not been demolished per all opened sources.
